narrative:
First Warn Forecast
Monday, June 15, 2026
Headlines

Quiet weather continues through Tuesday before a potentially significant severe weather threat develops Wednesday.

All severe weather hazards may be possible Wednesday afternoon and evening, including damaging winds, large hail, and tornadoes.

Forecast confidence is increasing that portions of Missouri and Illinois could experience an impactful severe weather event, but important details remain uncertain.

A stretch of quiet and comfortable weather will continue across Missouri and Illinois through Tuesday before attention turns toward what could become a significant severe weather event on Wednesday. While confidence remains high that thunderstorms will develop somewhere across the Mid-Mississippi Valley, questions remain regarding the exact timing, placement, and intensity of storms. Those details will depend heavily on how the atmosphere evolves during the morning and early afternoon hours Wednesday.

Today will feature pleasant conditions with sunshine, low humidity, and temperatures climbing into the upper 70s and lower 80s. High pressure remains in control of the weather pattern, providing a welcome break from both heat and storm activity. Overnight temperatures will fall back into the 50s, with a few areas of patchy fog possible in river valleys and low-lying locations.

Tuesday will mark the beginning of a transition toward a more active pattern. A cold front approaching from the north will bring increasing cloud cover and the possibility of scattered showers and perhaps a thunderstorm or two. However, moisture and instability remain somewhat limited, which should keep the severe weather threat low. Most locations will simply experience partly sunny skies with temperatures reaching the lower 80s.

Forecast attention remains focused on Wednesday as a powerful storm system moves across the Upper Midwest. A warm front is expected to lift northward through Missouri and Illinois during the morning while strong southerly winds transport rich Gulf moisture into the region. At the same time, a strengthening low-level jet and increasing upper-level winds will create an environment highly supportive of organized severe thunderstorms.

Current forecast data suggests atmospheric instability will increase substantially Wednesday afternoon, while wind shear values become unusually strong for mid-June. This combination is favorable for supercell thunderstorms capable of producing all forms of severe weather. Forecast models indicate moderate to strong instability developing across much of Missouri and Illinois, combined with wind fields capable of supporting rotating storms.

The largest uncertainty involves what occurs during the morning hours on Wednesday. Some forecast guidance develops clusters of showers and thunderstorms before sunrise and through the morning. If those storms become widespread and linger through midday, they may limit how much instability can recover later in the day. Conversely, if morning activity is more isolated or exits the area early enough, the atmosphere could become primed for a much more significant severe weather outbreak by afternoon and evening.

At this time, all severe weather hazards appear possible. Discrete supercells may initially develop during the afternoon, bringing the threat of large hail and tornadoes. As storms mature and interact with one another, they may organize into a line of severe thunderstorms capable of producing widespread damaging winds. The overall environment would also support the potential for strong tornadoes if individual storms can remain isolated before storm modes transition later in the day.

The Storm Prediction Center continues to highlight the Middle Mississippi Valley for an all-hazards severe weather episode. Forecast discussions reference the potential for intense thunderstorms capable of producing swaths of damaging wind, large hail, and strong tornadoes from portions of Missouri and Illinois eastward into Indiana. Very strong winds throughout the atmosphere, abundant moisture, and increasing instability are all expected to be in place as the cold front approaches Wednesday afternoon and evening.

The highest threat window currently appears to be from late Wednesday afternoon through Wednesday night. Residents across the St. Louis metro area, central Missouri, southeast Missouri, and southern Illinois should closely monitor forecasts over the next two days. While uncertainty remains regarding the ultimate magnitude of the event, the ingredients are present for a potentially significant severe weather episode somewhere within the region.

Following the passage of the cold front, showers and thunderstorms may linger into Thursday, especially across southern and eastern portions of the region. Cooler and less humid air is expected to return by the end of the week, bringing a return to quieter weather conditions and temperatures near or slightly below seasonal averages.

We will continue refining the forecast as confidence increases in the placement and timing of Wednesday's severe weather threat. Now is the time to review severe weather safety plans, ensure multiple ways to receive warnings are available, and stay informed as this potentially impactful weather event approaches.
